title: New ad accounts need months of pixel training before expecting results
insight: Businesses that have never run Meta ads or installed a tracking pixel should expect several months before ads start performing. Meta needs time to build conversion profiles: it must observe who visits your site, who buys, and what behaviors correlate with purchases. Without this training data, the algorithm can't find your audience effectively. New advertisers should install the pixel immediately, set up the Conversions API, and budget the first quarter purely for algorithm training — not for ROAS. Expecting immediate returns from a cold pixel is the #1 cause of 'ads don't work for me' conclusions.
raw_excerpt: If you've never run ads before, if you've never added a tracking pixel to your website before, you're going to have to give it a few months for things to start working... allow Meta to see who comes and goes to your site, who buys from you, and then it can start to build up a profile.
source_title: Marketing Made Social: How to Plan Your Paid Ads for 2026
actionable_steps: Install pixel and Conversions API immediately, even if you're not planning to run ads for months
Upload customer lists and email lists to Meta to accelerate profile building
Budget the first 2-3 months as algorithm training — measure engagement signals, not ROAS
If you can't sell organically yet, fix that first before investing in paid acquisition

title: Ads should amplify proven offers during peak demand — not rescue slow periods
insight: A common mistake is increasing ad spend during slow sales periods to compensate for dipping revenue. Ads cannot create demand that doesn't exist — they amplify existing demand. A knitwear brand should spend more on ads in winter when demand is high, not in summer trying to force sales. Seasonal businesses should map their best-selling periods from historical data (Shopify/WooCommerce analytics) and concentrate ad budget there. Slow months should be used for lead generation and list building to fuel the next peak period.
raw_excerpt: You can't sell everything all the time. Use ads to amplify when those products are more likely to sell. Not try and force an offer during the quieter times with ads when it just won't work... So you can't expect ads to fix slow months.
source_title: Marketing Made Social: How to Plan Your Paid Ads for 2026
actionable_steps: Map historical sales data to identify peak and trough months for each product/offer
Concentrate ad budget during peak demand periods rather than spreading evenly across the year
Use slow periods for lead gen and email list building to fuel the next peak
Don't increase ad spend reactively when sales dip — diagnose whether it's a demand problem or a creative problem
